threats.title: Fully Predictable Entropy Enables Selective High-Value Token Minting for Maximum NukeFund Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: All entropy values are deterministic (derived from block.number at batch-write time), stored once, and publicly readable via getPublicEntropy(). Combined with publicly readable currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex, any user can predict exactly which entropy the next mint will yield. An attacker can calculate all 10,010 entropy values, identify the positions of the highest-value tokens (high nuke factor, 999999 alpha), and time their mints to capture them. On Base (no public mempool), the attacker simply reads on-chain state, waits for the right entropy index, and calls mintToken.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the fundamental fairness of the NFT game. The entropy determines nuke factor (ETH payout), forge potential, and role. If one player can select the best tokens while others mint blindly, the informed player systematically extracts disproportionate value from the NukeFund at the expense of all other participants.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getPublicEntropy is public view returning getEntropy for any slot/number. currentSlotIndex and currentNumberIndex are private but inferable. Entire entropy sequence is deterministic from block.number at write time.

preconditions: Entropy batches have been written (writeEntropyBatch1/2/3 called)
Minting is active (not paused, whitelist passed or expired)
Attacker can read on-chain state (getPublicEntropy, slot/number indices)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: getEntropy(slotIndex, numberIndex) fully deterministic; getPublicEntropy exposes it
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker computes all 10,010 entropy values off-chain, identifies tokens with highest initialNukeFactor (entropy/40) and the 999999 alpha token position
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Alpha token (999999) gives initialNukeFactor=24999, claimable ~25% of fund. High-entropy tokens (e.g., 900000+) give ~22500 nukeFactor for ~22.5% of fund per nuke.

economic_questions: What is the distribution of entropy values across 10,010 slots?
How many tokens yield >20000 initialNukeFactor (top tier)?
What is the expected fund size when high-value tokens become nukeable?
validation_requirements: Simulate entropy generation and identify all high-value positions
Calculate total minting cost to reach high-value positions vs nuke payout
Verify attacker can reliably predict currentSlotIndex/currentNumberIndex

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minting cost to reach desired entropy index (varies; early indices cost ~0.005-0.25 ETH each)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Standard gas for mintToken calls on Base
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 25% of NukeFund per alpha token; 10-22% per high-entropy token
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Dependent on fund size at nuke time, but ROI can be >1000% on mint cost
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block: mint strategically, wait minimumDaysHeld, then nuke

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Mint when entropy index aligns with high-value slot; nuke after fund accumulates
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; pure on-chain state reading

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other NFT holders who mint with inferior entropy
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced NukeFund share after informed player extracts disproportionate value
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Up to 50% of NukeFund per strategic nuke

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Map entropy value distribution, identify optimal mint positions

threats.title: Generation Increment Permanently Blocked by Access Control Mismatch on initializeAlphaIndices
threats.hypothesis_description: TraitForgeNft._incrementGeneration() calls entropyGenerator.initializeAlphaIndices(), which has an onlyOwner modifier. The EntropyGenerator's owner is the deployer, not the TraitForgeNft contract. The deployment script sets TraitForgeNft as allowedCaller but never transfers ownership of EntropyGenerator to TraitForgeNft. When the 10,000th token of generation 1 is minted, _incrementGeneration reverts, permanently blocking all further minting.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a protocol-breaking bug that halts all minting after generation 1. The entire economic model (10 generations, 100,000 tokens) is reduced to a single generation. All accumulated NukeFund ETH from gen-1 mints remains, but the protocol cannot progress.
deployment_assumptions: EntropyGenerator ownership is NOT transferred to TraitForgeNft in deployment
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts: entropyGenerator deployed with nft as allowedCaller, but no transferOwnership call. initializeAlphaIndices uses onlyOwner, not onlyAllowedCaller.

proof_of_signal.pattern: initializeAlphaIndices() has onlyOwner modifier; called by TraitForgeNft._incrementGeneration() at TraitForgeNft.sol:261, but TraitForgeNft is allowedCaller not owner

preconditions: EntropyGenerator owner != TraitForgeNft (confirmed by deployment script)
generationMintCounts[1] reaches maxTokensPerGen (10000)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: onlyOwner on initializeAlphaIndices vs onlyAllowedCaller expected
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: No manipulation needed - the 10,000th mint of gen 1 automatically triggers the revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Protocol halts: no gen 2+ minting, no generation-based price curve, reduced NukeFund accumulation

economic_questions: What is the total ETH locked in NukeFund at gen-1 completion?
Can the owner manually fix by transferring ownership mid-flight?
Does this affect the 10,000th minter's transaction (DoS at boundary)?
validation_requirements: Verify EntropyGenerator owner after deployment is deployer, not NFT contract
Test minting the 10,000th token to confirm revert
Check if owner can call transferOwnership on EntropyGenerator as a fix

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A - this is a protocol failure, not attacker profit

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: 90% of intended protocol functionality and revenue

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Call _incrementGeneration in test environment to confirm revert

threats.title: Uninitialized ageMultiplier Nullifies Age-Based Nuke Factor Making NukeFund Exploitable Immediately
threats.hypothesis_description: NukeFund.ageMultiplier defaults to 0 and the deployment script never calls setAgeMultplier(). calculateAge() returns (daysOld * perfomanceFactor * MAX_DENOMINATOR * 0) / 365 = 0 for all tokens. This means nuke factor is purely entropy/40, completely ignoring hold time. Users can nuke tokens immediately after minimumDaysHeld (3 days) for the same payout as holding for years, undermining the core hold-to-earn mechanic.
threats.investigation_rationale: The age-based nuke factor increase is the primary economic incentive to hold tokens. With ageMultiplier=0, the economic model collapses to a simple lottery based on entropy. High-entropy token holders extract maximum value with no holding period beyond the minimum 3 days.
deployment_assumptions: setAgeMultplier is never called after deployment
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts: No call to nukeFund.setAgeMultplier() anywhere in the deployment script. NukeFund.sol constructor does not set ageMultiplier.

proof_of_signal.pattern: calculateAge multiplies by ageMultiplier which is 0 by default: age = (daysOld * perfomanceFactor * MAX_DENOMINATOR * ageMultiplier) / 365; ageMultiplier=0 makes entire expression 0

preconditions: ageMultiplier == 0 (default, confirmed by deployment script gap)
NukeFund has accumulated ETH from minting
Token held for >= minimumDaysHeld (3 days)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: calculateAge always returns 0; calculateNukeFactor = 0 + entropy/40 = entropy/40
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Combined with H001 (predictable entropy), attacker mints highest-entropy tokens and nukes after 3 days for max payout
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Token with entropy 999999: nukeFactor = 24999, claimAmount = fund * 24999/100000 ≈ 25% of fund after just 3 days

economic_questions: What is the expected fund size after 3 days of minting?
How does entropy/40 distribution look across all 10,010 positions?
Is the 3-day minimum sufficient to prevent immediate extraction?
validation_requirements: Confirm ageMultiplier is 0 in deployed state
Calculate nuke payouts for top-10 entropy values at various fund sizes
Compare intended age-based accrual vs actual entropy-only payouts

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minting cost for high-entropy tokens (~0.005-0.25 ETH each)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 25% of NukeFund per high-entropy token nuked
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: >500% ROI on mint cost after 3-day hold

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After fund has accumulated significant ETH from other minters
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Fund rapidly drained by early strategic nukers

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders expecting age-based value accrual
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced fund balance for late nukers
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire NukeFund balance at risk of early extraction

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm calculateAge returns 0 with default ageMultiplier

threats.title: mintWithBudget Permanently Non-Functional After Generation 1 Due to Wrong Loop Condition
threats.hypothesis_description: mintWithBudget uses `_tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen` as the while loop condition. _tokenIds is a global token counter that only increases across all generations, while maxTokensPerGen is 10,000. After generation 1 completes (10,000 tokens minted), _tokenIds >= 10,000 and the loop condition is permanently false. All ETH sent to mintWithBudget in gen 2+ is immediately refunded without minting.
threats.investigation_rationale: mintWithBudget is the primary batch-minting function. Its permanent failure after gen 1 forces users to call mintToken individually, increasing gas costs significantly and degrading UX. This may also cause users to lose budgeted strategies.

proof_of_signal.pattern: while (budgetLeft >= mintPrice && _tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen) - should use generationMintCounts[currentGeneration] < maxTokensPerGen

preconditions: Generation 1 has completed (10,000 tokens minted)
_tokenIds >= 10,000
User calls mintWithBudget for generation 2+

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attack but a code defect - entire batch minting pathway disabled
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users forced to use single-mint at higher gas cost; protocol loses batch-mint convenience

economic_questions: What percentage of users would use mintWithBudget vs mintToken?
What is the gas cost differential per token between batch and single mint?
Does this affect generation transition behavior?
validation_requirements: Test mintWithBudget after 10,000 mints to confirm no tokens minted
Verify all ETH is refunded
Confirm mintToken still works for gen 2+

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - code defect, not an attack

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Major UX degradation for batch minters

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users attempting batch minting in gen 2+
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Increased gas costs for individual mints; wasted gas on failed mintWithBudget calls

threats.title: Off-by-One in Forge Potential Check Allows Extra Forge Per Token
threats.hypothesis_description: listForForging checks `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential` using inclusive comparison. Since forgingCounts starts at 0, a forger with forgePotential=N can list when count=N (N<=N passes), then forge (count becomes N+1). This allows N+1 forges instead of N. The forger's count is NOT rechecked in forgeWithListed, only incremented.
threats.investigation_rationale: Each extra forge creates an additional NFT in the next generation, inflating supply beyond intended limits. The forger also collects an extra forging fee. For high-forge-potential tokens, this is a minor percentage increase, but for forgePotential=1 tokens, it doubles the forging capacity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential allows listing when count equals potential; forgeWithListed line 115 increments forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]++ without checking against forgePotential

preconditions: Token is a forger (entropy % 3 == 0)
forgePotential > 0
forgingCounts equals forgePotential (at the boundary)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: List at count=forgePotential, forge to exceed limit by 1
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extra forging fee earned per token + extra NFT minted

economic_questions: What is the distribution of forgePotential values across tokens?
How much extra forging fee can be extracted per token?
What is the cumulative impact across all forger tokens?
validation_requirements: Test listing when forgingCounts == forgePotential
Verify forge succeeds and count exceeds potential
Calculate total extra forges across all tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: One extra forging fee per token (0.01+ ETH minimum)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Gradual supply inflation from extra forged tokens

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol economy - excess token supply dilutes NukeFund payouts
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Moderate - depends on how many tokens exploit the extra forge

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: forgePotential distribution from entropy, minimumListFee=0.01 ETH

threats.title: Excess ETH Permanently Locked in EntityForging Due to Missing Refund
threats.hypothesis_description: forgeWithListed checks `msg.value >= forgingFee` but does not refund excess ETH. Any amount above the forging fee remains permanently locked in the EntityForging contract, which has no withdrawal function. Unlike mintToken which refunds excess, forgeWithListed simply ignores overpayment.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users who send excess ETH when forging lose funds permanently. While individual losses may be small, cumulative locked ETH represents a measurable economic loss with no recovery path.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(msg.value >= forgingFee) with no refund of (msg.value - forgingFee); contrast with TraitForgeNft.mintToken lines 168-171 which refund excess

preconditions: User sends msg.value > forgingFee to forgeWithListed
No withdraw function exists on EntityForging

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Only forgingFee is distributed (devFee + forgerShare = forgingFee); excess stays in contract
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Accidental overpayment by users results in permanent fund loss
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit, but permanent user fund loss

economic_questions: How often do users send excess ETH in forging transactions?
What is the expected cumulative locked ETH over protocol lifetime?
Can a withdrawal function be added retroactively?
validation_requirements: Send excess ETH to forgeWithListed and verify it's locked
Confirm no withdrawal or rescue function exists
Test contract balance accumulation over multiple forges with excess

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - user loss, not attacker exploit

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any user who overpays forging fee
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative locked ETH over protocol lifetime

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify contract balance grows with excess payments

threats.title: No Access Control on writeEntropyBatch Functions Allows Adversarial Entropy Seeding
threats.hypothesis_description: writeEntropyBatch1(), writeEntropyBatch2(), and writeEntropyBatch3() have no access control. Anyone can call them. Entropy values are derived from block.number at call time. An attacker who calls these functions at a strategically chosen block controls the entire entropy sequence for all 10,010 tokens. Between contract deployment and the deployer's batch-write transactions, there is a window where an attacker can front-run the batch writes.
threats.investigation_rationale: If an attacker writes the entropy batches, they know all entropy values before anyone else (since they chose the block.number). Combined with selective minting (H001), this gives them complete information advantage over all other players.
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts: writeEntropyBatch1/2/3 called as separate transactions with await/wait between each, creating windows

proof_of_signal.pattern: writeEntropyBatch1 is public with no onlyOwner/onlyAllowedCaller modifier. Same for writeEntropyBatch2 (lines 64-76) and writeEntropyBatch3 (lines 79-94)

preconditions: EntropyGenerator deployed but batches not yet written
Attacker observes deployment and calls batch functions before deployer
On Base: attacker reacts in next block after seeing deployment confirmation

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: entropy = keccak256(block.number, i) % 10^78; block.number chosen by attacker's tx timing
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls writeEntropyBatch1/2/3 at chosen block, knows all resulting entropy values
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Complete information advantage: attacker pre-computes all token values and mints only the best

economic_questions: What is the time window between deployment and batch writing?
Can an attacker on Base react within 1-2 blocks to call batch functions?
How much does block.number choice affect entropy distribution quality?
validation_requirements: Verify no access control on batch functions
Measure time window in deployment script between deploy and batch write
Test calling batch functions from non-deployer account

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.01-0.1 ETH in gas (large transactions writing 770 slots)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Complete game information advantage; profit from selective minting
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on fund size and token selection strategy

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after contract deployment, before deployer writes batches

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All future minters who don't know the entropy was adversarially seeded
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Integrity of entire token value system

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm batch functions are callable by any address

threats.title: Entropy Value Recycling Across Generations Makes All Future Tokens Predictable
threats.hypothesis_description: getNextEntropy() wraps currentSlotIndex back to 0 after reaching maxSlotIndex-1 (769). With 770 slots × 13 numbers = 10,010 entropy values and 10,000 tokens per generation, entropy values are exhausted within ~1 generation. All subsequent generations reuse the same entropy values. Since _incrementGeneration only resets alpha indices (slotIndexSelectionPoint/numberIndexSelectionPoint), not the entropy data, tokens in gen 2+ have identical entropy to gen 1 tokens at the same index.
threats.investigation_rationale: Combined with the public predictability of entropy (H001), this means that after observing generation 1, all future generation entropies are known with certainty. Players who tracked gen 1 have a permanent information advantage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: currentSlotIndex wraps to 0 when >= maxSlotIndex-1; entropySlots[] never regenerated. _incrementGeneration only calls initializeAlphaIndices (new 999999 position) but not new entropy data.

preconditions: Generation 2+ is reachable (requires H002 to be fixed)
10,010+ tokens have been minted, exhausting entropy pool
Attacker observed or computed gen 1 entropy values

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: 770 slots × 13 values = 10,010 unique entropies for 10,000 per gen × 10 gens = 100,000 tokens
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After gen 1, attacker knows exact entropy for every position in gen 2+
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Guaranteed selection of highest-value tokens in every generation

economic_questions: Are there exactly 10,010 unique entropy values for 100,000 potential tokens?
Does entropy index wrap cleanly at generation boundaries?
How does recycled entropy interact with different generation pricing?
validation_requirements: Track entropy index across generation boundary in test
Verify entropy values repeat identically
Map the 10 extra values (10,010 - 10,000) and their generation alignment

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Same as H001 but with 100% certainty in gen 2+

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Permanent information asymmetry between informed and uninformed players

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Fair value distribution across all generations

threats.title: currentGeneration Can Exceed maxGeneration Cap Through Minting Overflow
threats.hypothesis_description: _incrementGeneration increments currentGeneration without checking if it exceeds maxGeneration. When generation 10 (maxGeneration) fills up, _incrementGeneration sets currentGeneration to 11. Subsequent mints use generation 11, exceeding the intended 10-generation cap. While forge() checks newGeneration <= maxGeneration, _mintInternal does not.
threats.investigation_rationale: This violates the documented protocol invariant of maximum 10 generations. Tokens minted in generation 11+ may have unpredictable properties and dilute the NukeFund beyond intended limits. However, this requires H002 to be fixed first.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _incrementGeneration has no require(currentGeneration < maxGeneration). After currentGeneration++, value can exceed maxGeneration. Contrast with forge() which checks newGeneration <= maxGeneration.

preconditions: H002 is fixed (generation increment works)
All 10 generations fill up (100,000 tokens minted)
User mints the last token of generation 10

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural minting progression pushes generation past cap
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Unlimited token supply beyond 100,000 cap; fund dilution

economic_questions: Can tokens be minted indefinitely past gen 10?
How does generation 11+ affect pricing curve?
Does this affect NukeFund payout calculations?
validation_requirements: Test _incrementGeneration when currentGeneration == maxGeneration
Verify _mintInternal continues to work in gen 11+
Check if priceIncrement continues accumulating

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Unlimited token supply devalues all existing tokens

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Token supply inflation beyond design limits

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Diluted NukeFund share from excess token supply

threats.title: taxCut Set to Zero Causes Division-by-Zero Bricking All Protocol ETH Flows
threats.hypothesis_description: NukeFund.receive() calculates `devShare = msg.value / taxCut`. EntityForging.forgeWithListed calculates `devFee = forgingFee / taxCut`. EntityTrading.buyNFT calculates `nukeFundContribution = msg.value / taxCut`. If any owner sets taxCut to 0 via the unrestricted setTaxCut function, all three contracts experience division-by-zero reverts, halting minting, trading, and forging.
threats.investigation_rationale: setTaxCut has no lower bound validation. A single owner misconfiguration across any of the three contracts bricks its ETH distribution. Since minting sends all ETH to NukeFund, a taxCut=0 on NukeFund halts the entire protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: devShare = msg.value / taxCut with no require(taxCut > 0) in setTaxCut. Same pattern in EntityForging.sol:109 and EntityTrading.sol:63

preconditions: Owner calls setTaxCut(0) on NukeFund, EntityForging, or EntityTrading
Any ETH-receiving function is subsequently called

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: msg.value / taxCut where taxCut can be 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner misconfiguration or malicious action sets taxCut=0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Complete protocol halt - no new mints, trades, or forges possible

economic_questions: Can the owner recover by setting taxCut back to non-zero?
What is the blast radius of taxCut=0 on each contract?
Are there pending transactions that would revert?
validation_requirements: Call setTaxCut(0) and verify subsequent operations revert
Verify setTaxCut(N>0) restores functionality
Check if taxCut=1 causes 100% dev share (0% to fund)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: taxCut default=10, setter has no lower bound
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify division by zero revert on taxCut=0

threats.title: Missing Pause/Unpause Functions Prevent Emergency Response to Active Exploits
threats.hypothesis_description: All six contracts inherit OpenZeppelin's Pausable and use whenNotPaused on critical functions, but none exposes external pause() or unpause() functions. OpenZeppelin's _pause() and _unpause() are internal functions. Without public wrappers, the pause mechanism is completely non-functional. If any vulnerability (e.g., H001, H003) is being actively exploited, the owner cannot pause the protocol to prevent further damage.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical operational security gap. The whenNotPaused modifiers create a false sense of security. In an active exploit scenario, the inability to pause allows attackers to continue extracting value while the team scrambles for a fix.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Contract inherits Pausable and uses whenNotPaused modifiers, but no function calls _pause() or _unpause() anywhere in any of the 6 contracts

preconditions: Active exploit in progress
Owner attempts to pause protocol
No external pause() function exists

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker continues exploiting while pause mechanism is non-functional
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Unbounded loss during exploit window; no emergency brake available

economic_questions: How much additional damage could an attacker cause without pause capability?
What alternative emergency mechanisms exist (if any)?
How long would it take to deploy a fix without pause?
validation_requirements: Search all contract source for _pause() or _unpause() calls
Confirm no external function exposes pause capability
Verify whenNotPaused modifiers are present but non-functional

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until a code fix is deployed

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Inability to contain damage from any vulnerability

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol participants during an exploit
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Depends on the specific exploit being amplified
